question: How does a sequential multi-instance activity differ from a parallel multi-instance activity in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Sequential creates one instance at a time and starts the next only after the previous completes; parallel creates all instances at activation and executes them concurrently and independently.
Sequential creates all instances up front but runs them in `inputCollection` order; parallel creates all instances up front and runs them in random order.
Sequential uses a single Zeebe stream processor while parallel spawns one OS thread per instance.
Sequential is the default; the parallel variant requires `isParallel="true"` to be set on the loop characteristics.

a.text: In a sequential multi-instance activity, instances are executed one at a time and a new instance for the next `inputCollection` element is created only when the current one completes. In a parallel multi-instance activity, all instances are created when the multi-instance body is activated and execute concurrently and independently.

b.text: Parallel multi-instance does not create all instances up front and then run them in random order; it creates all instances on activation and runs them concurrently. Sequential does not create them all up front at all.

c.text: Concurrency in Zeebe is not about OS threads. Records of a process instance are written to the same partition; the distinction is logical, not threading-related.

d.text: Parallel is the default. Sequential is selected by setting `isSequential="true"` on the multi-instance loop characteristics, not the other way around.

explanation: A sequential multi-instance executes instances one after another, creating each only when the previous finishes. A parallel multi-instance — the default — instantiates all instances when the body activates and runs them concurrently and independently. The BPMN notation reflects this: three horizontal lines for sequential, three vertical lines for parallel.
